nodes.name: Major Companies
nodes.description: Iconic companies and the people who built them

items.prompt: Who co-founded Apple Computer in a garage in Los Altos, California, in 1976?
items.explanation:
Co-founded April 1, 1976, by Steve Jobs, Steve Wozniak, and Ronald Wayne
First product was the Apple I, designed and hand-built entirely by Wozniak
Ronald Wayne held 10% but sold his stake back for $800 after 12 days
Jobs sold his VW Bus and Wozniak sold his HP-65 calculator to raise capital
Apple I retailed for $666.66; the Apple II (1977) launched the personal computer era
Jobs was ousted in 1985, returned in 1997, and led the iPod/iPhone/iPad revolution
First company to reach $1 trillion, $2 trillion, and $3 trillion market cap

items.prompt: Who co-founded Microsoft with Paul Allen in 1975?
items.explanation:
Co-founded April 4, 1975, by Bill Gates and Paul Allen in Albuquerque, New Mexico
Name coined by Allen: a portmanteau of 'microcomputer' and 'software'
First product was a BASIC interpreter for the MITS Altair 8800
MS-DOS deal with IBM in 1980 became the foundation of Microsoft's dominance
Windows launched in 1985; Windows 95 sold 7 million copies in five weeks
Gates was the world's richest person for much of 1995–2017
Allen died in 2018; owned the Portland Trail Blazers and Seattle Seahawks

items.prompt: Who founded Amazon as an online bookstore in his garage in Bellevue, Washington, in 1994?
items.explanation:
Founded July 5, 1994; launched online July 16, 1995, as an online bookstore
Bezos left a VP role at D.E. Shaw & Co. on Wall Street to start the company
Operated from a rented garage in Bellevue, WA, with desks made from doors
Original name was 'Cadabra'; changed to Amazon after the world's largest river
Amazon Web Services (AWS), launched 2006, now generates most of the company's profit
Bezos bought The Washington Post in 2013 for $250 million
Bezos founded Blue Origin (space company) in 2000 and flew to space in July 2021

items.prompt: Who co-founded Google with Sergey Brin as a Stanford University research project in 1998?
items.explanation:
Co-founded September 4, 1998, by Larry Page and Sergey Brin, PhD students at Stanford
Began as 'BackRub', a search engine ranking pages by backlinks (PageRank algorithm)
Incorporated in Susan Wojcicki's garage in Menlo Park, California
First outside investment was a $100,000 check from Sun co-founder Andy Bechtolsheim
Name derived from 'googol' (10^100), reflecting the mission to organize vast information
Parent company Alphabet created in 2015 restructuring
Brin emigrated from the Soviet Union at age 6; Page's parents were both computer science professors

items.prompt: Who launched Facebook from his Harvard dorm room on February 4, 2004?
items.explanation:
Launched February 4, 2004, as 'TheFacebook' from Zuckerberg's Harvard dorm room
Co-founded with Eduardo Saverin, Andrew McCollum, Dustin Moskovitz, and Chris Hughes
Half of Harvard's undergrads signed up within the first month
Zuckerberg dropped out of Harvard sophomore year; received an honorary degree in 2017
Rebranded parent company to Meta Platforms in October 2021
Acquired Instagram (2012, $1 billion) and WhatsApp (2014, $19 billion)
The Social Network (2010), directed by David Fincher, dramatized the founding story

items.prompt: Who incorporated Tesla Motors in San Carlos, California, in July 2003, before Elon Musk joined as lead investor?
items.explanation:
Incorporated July 1, 2003, by Martin Eberhard and Marc Tarpenning
Elon Musk joined in 2004, leading the $7.5M Series A round with $6.5M of his own money
A 2009 lawsuit settlement lets all five co-founders (Eberhard, Tarpenning, Wright, Musk, Straubel) use the title
Named after inventor Nikola Tesla; first product was the Roadster (2008), based on a Lotus Elise
Musk became CEO in 2008 after Eberhard was pushed out
Model S, Model 3, Model X, Model Y spell out S-3-X-Y (3 replaced E due to Ford's trademark)
Became the most valuable automaker by market cap in 2020

items.prompt: Who opened the first Walmart discount store in Rogers, Arkansas, in 1962?
items.explanation:
First Walmart opened July 2, 1962, in Rogers, Arkansas; Sam was 44 years old
Earlier ran Walton's 5&10 on the Bentonville, Arkansas, town square starting in 1950
Walmart became the world's largest company by revenue, a title it has held repeatedly
Walton's strategy: high volume, low margins, small-town locations competitors ignored
Went public in 1970 at $16.50 per share; made the Walton family the richest in America
Walton personally visited stores by flying his own plane and driving his old pickup truck
Died April 5, 1992; received the Presidential Medal of Freedom from George H.W. Bush the same year

items.prompt: Which brothers opened the original McDonald's restaurant in San Bernardino, California, in 1940?
items.explanation:
Richard ('Dick') and Maurice ('Mac') McDonald opened the original in San Bernardino, CA, 1940
Invented the 'Speedee Service System' (1948), the template for modern fast food
Ray Kroc, a milkshake mixer salesman, visited in 1954 and became their franchise agent
Kroc opened the first franchise in Des Plaines, Illinois, in April 1955
Kroc bought the company from the brothers in 1961 for $2.7 million
The brothers' original restaurant was not included in the deal; Kroc opened a McDonald's across the street
McDonald's now operates in 100+ countries with 40,000+ locations

items.prompt: Which brothers co-founded the Disney Brothers Cartoon Studio on October 16, 1923?
items.explanation:
Founded October 16, 1923, by Walt Disney and his brother Roy O. Disney
Originally called Disney Brothers Cartoon Studio; Walt insisted on 'Walt Disney Studio'
Walt's previous venture, Laugh-O-Gram Studio in Kansas City, had gone bankrupt
First success was the Alice Comedies, commissioned by New York distributor Margaret Winkler
Mickey Mouse debuted in Steamboat Willie (1928), one of the first synchronized sound cartoons
Snow White and the Seven Dwarfs (1937) was the first full-length animated feature film
Disneyland opened in Anaheim, CA, on July 17, 1955; Walt died of lung cancer in 1966

items.prompt: Who co-founded Blue Ribbon Sports (later Nike) with coach Bill Bowerman in 1964?
items.explanation:
Co-founded January 25, 1964, as Blue Ribbon Sports by Phil Knight and Bill Bowerman
Knight ran middle distance for Bowerman's track team at the University of Oregon
Started as a distributor for Japanese shoemaker Onitsuka Tiger (now ASICS)
Renamed Nike, Inc. on May 30, 1971, after the Greek goddess of victory
Bowerman invented the waffle sole by pouring rubber into his wife's waffle iron
The Swoosh logo was designed by Portland State student Carolyn Davidson for $35 in 1971
Knight's memoir Shoe Dog (2016) details the company's scrappy early years

items.prompt: Who founded the Ford Motor Company, incorporated on June 16, 1903, in Detroit?
items.explanation:
Ford Motor Company incorporated June 16, 1903, with $28,000 from 12 investors
The first president was actually banker John S. Gray, not Ford himself
Model T (1908) made cars affordable; Ford sold 15 million by 1927
Introduced the moving assembly line at Highland Park in 1913, cutting chassis assembly from 12 hours to 93 minutes
Famous $5 workday (1914) doubled prevailing wages and reduced turnover
Ford was a controversial figure: received the Grand Cross of the German Eagle from Nazi Germany in 1938
Died April 7, 1947; Ford Motor remained family-controlled through a dual-class stock structure

items.prompt: Who founded Standard Oil in Cleveland, Ohio, in 1870, becoming America's first billionaire?
items.explanation:
Standard Oil of Ohio incorporated January 10, 1870, with $1 million in capital
Co-founded with brother William, Samuel Andrews, Henry Flagler, and Stephen Harkness
Controlled ~90% of U.S. oil refining by the late 1870s through aggressive acquisitions
Broken up by the Supreme Court in 1911 under the Sherman Antitrust Act into 34 companies
Spinoffs include ExxonMobil, Chevron, and BP America (Sohio)
Rockefeller is often cited as the richest American in history (inflation-adjusted wealth ~$400 billion)
Founded the University of Chicago (1890) and Rockefeller Foundation (1913)

items.prompt: Who took control of the struggling textile company Berkshire Hathaway in 1965 and transformed it into an investment conglomerate?
items.explanation:
Buffett began buying Berkshire shares in 1962 at $7.50/share through his investment partnership
Took control May 10, 1965, originally out of spite after a lowball tender offer
Berkshire Hathaway was a failing New England textile maker; last textile mill closed in 1985
Buffett has called buying the textile company 'the biggest investment mistake' of his career
Class A shares have never split; traded above $600,000/share by 2024
Owns GEICO, BNSF Railway, Dairy Queen, See's Candies, and large stakes in Apple and Coca-Cola
Buffett pledged to give away 99% of his wealth; annual shareholder letter is widely read

items.prompt: Who founded Samsung as a trading company in Daegu, Korea, on March 1, 1938?
items.explanation:
Founded March 1, 1938, in Daegu, as a small trading company exporting dried fish and noodles
Samsung means 'three stars' in Korean, symbolizing something big, numerous, and powerful
Diversified into textiles, insurance, securities, and retail over the next three decades
Entered electronics in the late 1960s; Samsung Electronics established in 1969
Now the world's largest manufacturer of smartphones and memory chips
The Lee family has controlled Samsung through a complex cross-shareholding structure
Lee Byung-chul died in 1987; son Lee Kun-hee led the company until his death in 2020

items.prompt: Who co-founded Tokyo Tsushin Kogyo (later Sony) with Masaru Ibuka in 1946?
items.explanation:
Founded May 7, 1946, as Tokyo Tsushin Kogyo (Tokyo Telecommunications Engineering Corp.)
Co-founded by Masaru Ibuka and Akio Morita; they met in a wartime navy research group
Started in a bombed-out department store in Tokyo with about 20 employees
Built Japan's first tape recorder (Type-G) and first transistor radio (TR-55, 1955)
Renamed Sony Corporation in 1958; name from Latin 'sonus' (sound) and English 'sonny'
Walkman (1979) created the portable music category and sold 200+ million units
Morita's heir to a sake-brewing dynasty; Ibuka was the engineer, Morita the marketer
